summary: The NA Miata is legendary for its simplicity and reliability. The 1.6L and 1.8L engines are nearly bulletproof when maintained. At 30+ years old, age-related issues dominate, not engineering flaws.

issues.name: Rust
issues.description: The NA Miata's biggest enemy is rust. Rocker panels, fenders, trunk lid, and the drain holes behind the rear wheels are the most common areas.

issues.name: Soft Top Deterioration
issues.description: The cloth convertible top degrades with UV exposure and age. The rear plastic window yellows, cracks, and eventually fails.

issues.name: Short Nose Crank (1990–1991 1.6L)
issues.description: The 1990–1991 1.6L engines have a shorter crankshaft nose that can shear the keyway, causing timing loss and potential valve damage.

issues.name: CAS (Crank Angle Sensor) O-Ring Leak
issues.description: The CAS O-ring hardens and leaks oil into the sensor, causing intermittent ignition problems and possible engine stalling.

issues.name: Worn Suspension Bushings
issues.description: At 30+ years old, every rubber bushing in the suspension is likely deteriorated, causing sloppy handling and clunking over bumps.

buyingTips: Rust is the number one killer of NA Miatas — inspect rocker panels, fenders, and the trunk floor with a flashlight
The 1.8L engine (1994+) is preferred — it has more power and avoids the short nose crank issue of early 1.6L models
Check the timing belt service history — the 1.6L and 1.8L are non-interference, but a broken belt still leaves you stranded
Every rubber component is likely original and worn — budget for a full bushing, seal, and hose refresh
The NA Miata is appreciating in value — clean, low-rust examples command premium prices
